title: Factor polynomials using structure
description: Factor "advanced" polynomials (i.e. polynomials of various degrees and or with two variables) using special product factorization methods.

hints:
Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{4x})^2 - (\greenD{7y})^2 =(\blueD{4x}+\greenD{7y})(\blueD{4x}-\greenD{7y})$

In conclusion,

$16x^2-49y^2=(4x+7y)(4x-7y)$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{6 c })^2 - (\greenD{11 d })^2 =(\blueD{6 c }+\greenD{11 d })(\blueD{6 c }-\greenD{11 d })$

In conclusion,

$36 c ^2-121 d ^2=(6 c +11 d )(6 c -11 d )$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{2 p })^2 - (\greenD{5 q })^2 =(\blueD{2 p }+\greenD{5 q })(\blueD{2 p }-\greenD{5 q })$

In conclusion,

$4 p ^2-25 q ^2=(2 p +5 q )(2 p -5 q )$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}9m^2+30mn+25n^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{3m})^2+2(\blueD{3m})(\greenD{5n})+(\greenD{5n})^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{3m})^2+2(\blueD{3m})(\greenD{5n})+(\greenD{5n})^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{3m}+\greenD{5n})^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$9m^2+30mn+25n^2=(3m+5n)^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}4 x ^2 + 36 x  y + 81 y ^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{2 x })^2 + 2(\blueD{2 x })(\greenD{9 y })+(\greenD{9 y })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{2 x })^2 + 2(\blueD{2 x })(\greenD{9 y })+(\greenD{9 y })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{2 x } + \greenD{9 y })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$4 x ^2 + 36 x  y + 81 y ^2=(2 x  + 9 y )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}49 c ^2 + 70 c  d + 25 d ^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{7 c })^2 + 2(\blueD{7 c })(\greenD{5 d })+(\greenD{5 d })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{7 c })^2 + 2(\blueD{7 c })(\greenD{5 d })+(\greenD{5 d })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{7 c } + \greenD{5 d })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$49 c ^2 + 70 c  d + 25 d ^2=(7 c  + 5 d )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}81 p ^2 - 144 p  q + 64 q ^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{9 p })^2 - 2(\blueD{9 p })(\greenD{8 q })+(\greenD{8 q })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{9 p })^2 - 2(\blueD{9 p })(\greenD{8 q })+(\greenD{8 q })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{9 p } - \greenD{8 q })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$81 p ^2 - 144 p  q + 64 q ^2=(9 p  - 8 q )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}49 m ^2 - 126 m  n + 81 n ^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{7 m })^2 - 2(\blueD{7 m })(\greenD{9 n })+(\greenD{9 n })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{7 m })^2 - 2(\blueD{7 m })(\greenD{9 n })+(\greenD{9 n })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{7 m } - \greenD{9 n })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$49 m ^2 - 126 m  n + 81 n ^2=(7 m  - 9 n )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}36 c ^2 - 84 c  d + 49 d ^2
\\\\
&= (\blueD{6 c })^2 - 2(\blueD{6 c })(\greenD{7 d })+(\greenD{7 d })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{6 c })^2 - 2(\blueD{6 c })(\greenD{7 d })+(\greenD{7 d })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{6 c } - \greenD{7 d })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$36 c ^2 - 84 c  d + 49 d ^2=(6 c  - 7 d )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{4x^3})^2 - (\greenD{9})^2 =(\blueD{4x^3}+\greenD{9})(\blueD{4x^3}-\greenD{9})$

In conclusion,

$16x^6-81=(4x^3+9)(4x^3-9)$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{9 p ^4})^2 - (\greenD{10  })^2 =(\blueD{9 p ^4}+\greenD{10  })(\blueD{9 p ^4}-\greenD{10  })$

In conclusion,

$81 p ^8-100  =(9 p ^4+10  )(9 p ^4-10  )$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

Using the *difference of squares* pattern:

$(\blueD{7 q ^2})^2 - (\greenD{2  })^2 =(\blueD{7 q ^2}+\greenD{2  })(\blueD{7 q ^2}-\greenD{2  })$

In conclusion,

$49 q ^4-4  =(7 q ^2+2  )(7 q ^2-2  )$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}49 m ^4 + 140 m ^2  + 100  
\\\\
&= (\blueD{7 m ^2})^2 + 2(\blueD{7 m ^2})(\greenD{10  })+(\greenD{10  })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{7 m ^2})^2 + 2(\blueD{7 m ^2})(\greenD{10  })+(\greenD{10  })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{7 m ^2} + \greenD{10  })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$49 m ^4 + 140 m ^2  + 100  =(7 m ^2 + 10  )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}16 n ^6 + 40 n ^3  + 25  
\\\\
&= (\blueD{4 n ^3})^2 + 2(\blueD{4 n ^3})(\greenD{5  })+(\greenD{5  })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{4 n ^3})^2 + 2(\blueD{4 n ^3})(\greenD{5  })+(\greenD{5  })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{4 n ^3} + \greenD{5  })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$16 n ^6 + 40 n ^3  + 25  =(4 n ^3 + 5  )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}49 p ^8 + 42 p ^4  + 9  
\\\\
&= (\blueD{7 p ^4})^2 + 2(\blueD{7 p ^4})(\greenD{3  })+(\greenD{3  })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a sum* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{7 p ^4})^2 + 2(\blueD{7 p ^4})(\greenD{3  })+(\greenD{3  })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{7 p ^4} + \greenD{3  })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$49 p ^8 + 42 p ^4  + 9  =(7 p ^4 + 3  )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}64y^6-48y^3+9
\\\\
&=(\blueD{8y^3})^2-2(\blueD{8y^3})(\greenD{3})+(\greenD{3})^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{8y^3})^2-2(\blueD{8y^3})(\greenD{3})+(\greenD{3})^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{8y^3}-\greenD{3})^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$64y^6-48y^3+9=(8y^3-3)^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}121 c ^4 - 44 c ^2  + 4  
\\\\
&= (\blueD{11 c ^2})^2 - 2(\blueD{11 c ^2})(\greenD{2  })+(\greenD{2  })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{11 c ^2})^2 - 2(\blueD{11 c ^2})(\greenD{2  })+(\greenD{2  })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{11 c ^2} - \greenD{2  })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$121 c ^4 - 44 c ^2  + 4  =(11 c ^2 - 2  )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}25 d ^8 - 80 d ^4  + 64  
\\\\
&= (\blueD{5 d ^4})^2 - 2(\blueD{5 d ^4})(\greenD{8  })+(\greenD{8  })^2
\end{align}$

Using the *square of a difference* pattern:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(\blueD{5 d ^4})^2 - 2(\blueD{5 d ^4})(\greenD{8  })+(\greenD{8  })^2
\\\\
&=(\blueD{5 d ^4} - \greenD{8  })^2
\end{align}$

In conclusion,

$25 d ^8 - 80 d ^4  + 64  =(5 d ^4 - 8  )^2$

*Remember that you can always check your factorization by expanding it.*
